sections.heading: Mission
sections.summary: Defines aoa-memo as the explicit memory and recall layer.
sections.body: `aoa-memo` is the explicit memory and recall layer of the AoA ecosystem. Its job is to make memory objects, provenance threads, temporal posture, and recall surfaces explicit, reviewable, and bounded.

sections.heading: Core rules
sections.summary: Names the layer's rules about memory, truth, provenance, and downstream graph work.
sections.body: Memory is not proof. Events come before claims. Provenance should stay visible. Temperature is not truth. Trust is multi-axial. Graph lifts are downstream. Live runtime state, role rights, and routing stay outside the repo.

sections.heading: Owns and does not own
sections.summary: Draws a hard line between memory-layer meaning and neighboring responsibilities.
sections.body: `aoa-memo` owns memory classes, provenance threads, recall contracts, salience and temperature posture, and public reviewable memory surfaces. It must not absorb reusable techniques, execution workflows, proof doctrine, routing logic, role policy, or runtime infrastructure.

sections.heading: Four axes
sections.summary: States the model's four axes: function, temperature, scope, and trust posture.
sections.body: `aoa-memo` uses four axes: function for what kind of remembering is happening, temperature for activation and recallability, scope for where a memory may matter, and trust posture for how it should be interpreted.

sections.heading: Memory functions
sections.summary: Distinguishes working, episodic, semantic, procedural-experience, associative, and audit memory.
sections.body: Working memory holds current state. Episodic memory records events. Semantic memory stores consolidated reusable statements. Procedural-experience memory remembers what worked or failed. Associative memory connects surfaces. Audit memory records lifecycle changes.

sections.heading: Temperature and KAG orientation
sections.summary: Defines temperature as recall posture and keeps KAG lifts downstream.
sections.body: Temperature describes recall posture rather than truth. Hot, warm, cool, cold, and frozen describe activation and stability. KAG orientation remains downstream: memory may expose chunk and graph faces, but `aoa-kag` owns normalization and substrate formation.

sections.heading: Source-of-truth rule
sections.summary: States that aoa-memo is the source of truth only for explicit memory-layer meaning.
sections.body: `aoa-memo` is the source of truth for explicit memory-layer meaning. It is not the source of truth for authored knowledge, execution, proof, routing, agent roles, scenario composition, or runtime infrastructure.

sections.heading: What belongs here
sections.summary: Lists in-bounds memory surfaces and objects.
sections.body: The repo should hold public reviewable memory objects and docs that clarify memory-layer meaning, including provenance-thread structure, validity windows, recall contracts, salience and temperature doctrine, generated registries and capsules, and validation surfaces.

sections.heading: What stays outside
sections.summary: Maps neighboring repos to their rightful ownership zones.
sections.body: ToS owns source-authored texts and lineage structure. Techniques, skills, evals, routing, agents, playbooks, KAG substrate work, and abyss-stack runtime concerns all stay outside aoa-memo and remain the responsibility of their own source repositories.

sections.heading: Direction and north star
sections.summary: Frames aoa-memo as a small, reviewable, provenance-aware, routeable memory layer.
sections.body: The direction is to keep `aoa-memo` explicit, provenance-aware, routeable, and KAG-ready without turning it into a runtime database, routing monolith, or graph platform. By v1.0 the layer should be clear, bounded, and integrable.

sections.heading: Milestones
sections.summary: Names the doctrine, schema, generated-surface, provenance, temperature, integration, KAG, eval, and v1.0 phases.
sections.body: The milestone sequence starts with doctrine and schemas, then generated public surfaces, provenance and lifecycle, temperature and consolidation, agent and playbook integration, KAG and ToS bridges, evaluation guardrails, and finally the stable doctrine-and-surface boundary at v1.0.

sections.heading: Cross-repo dependencies
sections.summary: Shows what aoa-routing, aoa-agents, aoa-kag, aoa-evals, abyss-stack, and ToS need from memo.
sections.body: `aoa-routing` needs a future memo kind. `aoa-agents` needs explicit memory posture fields. `aoa-kag` needs graph-ready exports. `aoa-evals` needs bounded checks. `abyss-stack` needs to own live stores and jobs. ToS needs bridge contracts rather than duplication.

sections.heading: Core rule
sections.summary: States that core memory is written once and downstream surfaces are derived later.
sections.body: Write the memory event once. Derive downstream surfaces later. `aoa-memo` owns explicit, reviewable memory surfaces and should not become a hidden graph platform, embedding empire, or substitute for Tree of Sophia.

sections.heading: Core memory surfaces
sections.summary: Keeps authored/core memory small, readable, and aligned with the shipped memory object taxonomy.
sections.body: The authored/core layer should stay small and reviewable. At the current baseline it follows the shipped `memory_object.schema.json` taxonomy and includes anchor, state_capsule, episode, claim, decision, pattern, bridge, and audit_event as direct inspectable surfaces.

sections.heading: Derived memory surfaces
sections.summary: Allows retrieval-oriented and graph-facing derivatives while keeping them downstream of authored/core memory.
sections.body: Derived memory may exist for retrieval, lift preparation, and downstream consumers, including chunk-oriented views, graph-facing relation candidates, embedding indexes, retrieval caches, KAG lift candidates, and framework adapters. These surfaces may accelerate recall, but they do not become the source of truth for memory meaning.

sections.heading: Ownership split
sections.summary: Assigns explicit responsibility across aoa-memo, aoa-kag, abyss-stack, and Tree of Sophia.
sections.body: `aoa-memo` owns explicit memory objects and provenance posture. `aoa-kag` owns normalized derived substrate and graph-ready projections. `abyss-stack` owns runtime stores, jobs, and storage topology. `Tree-of-Sophia` owns source-authored texts, concepts, and lineage architecture.

sections.heading: Required handoff rules
sections.summary: Requires source refs, provenance linkage, temporal posture, review state, and bounded derivation language in downstream handoffs.
sections.body: Any handoff from core memory into derived memory should preserve source refs, provenance thread linkage where available, temporal posture, review state, and a bounded statement of what was derived. Return paths from derived memory back into AoA should point toward stronger authored surfaces instead of presenting the derived surface as final truth.

sections.heading: Core rule
sections.summary: Keeps live scratchpad in runtime and restricts aoa-memo to bounded exports or reviewed writeback candidates.
sections.body: The runtime writeback seam keeps live scratchpad out of `aoa-memo`. The memo layer receives bounded exports or reviewed candidates, not the whole running state of a route.

sections.heading: Mapping to preserve
sections.summary: Maps checkpoint export, approval and transition records, traces, and distillation output into the existing memo canon.
sections.body: The preserved mapping is: checkpoint export becomes `state_capsule`; approval and transition records become `decision`; execution and review traces become `episode` and `audit_event`; `distillation_pack` yields reviewed candidates for `claim`, `pattern`, and `bridge`.

sections.heading: Boundary split
sections.summary: Keeps role rights in aoa-agents, scenario shape in aoa-playbooks, and runtime plumbing in abyss-stack.
sections.body: `aoa-memo` owns object canon, lifecycle, writeback classes, and review rules. `aoa-agents` keeps memory rights and writeback boundaries. `aoa-playbooks` keeps scenario meaning. `abyss-stack` keeps runtime stores, checkpoint workers, and writeback plumbing.
